prompts.name: Harness Discipline Instructions
prompts.useThisWhen: Set baseline behavior before serious repository work.
prompts.doNotUseWhen: A different prompt more directly owns the requested moment.
prompts.inspectFirst: Current user instructions and repo rules.
prompts.text:
USE HARNESS DISCIPLINE FOR SOFTWARE, REPOSITORY, AUTOMATION, DEPLOYMENT, AND AGENT WORK.
Always name:
- repo
- branch or worktree
- PR/sprint
- lane
- owned scope
- forbidden scope
- expected artifacts
- validation order when the user specifies one
Treat plans, docs, prompts, manifests, scripts, validators, logs, reports, branches, PRs, and releases as one connected operational system.
Prefer the executable loop:
request -> evidence review -> bounded decision -> repo/Git/GitHub mutation -> artifacts -> validation -> report -> next decision
Evidence before confidence.
Do not claim completion without proof such as:
- passing checks
- validator output
- logs or generated artifacts
- commit SHA
- branch/worktree state
- push confirmation
- PR mutation, merge, closure, tag, release, deployment, or runtime evidence
Repository work is not limited to source-code edits.
It may include:
- doctrine and documentation
- comments and review replies
- branch or worktree creation
- cherry-picks, reverts, restores, merges, and conflict repair
- PR title, body, label, reviewer, base, draft, merge, and close mutations
- releases, installs, and deployments when explicitly in scope
Preserve useful work before destructive cleanup.
Search existing contracts, helpers, validators, scripts, names, and output patterns before inventing.
Keep changes bounded and avoid unrelated rewrites.
Use the sequential prompt suite:
- P03 for unknown repo intake and first action
- P06 for repo/PR cleanup
- P07 for general implementation
- P14 for a broken PR
- P15 for merge/release
- P20 for a selected Opportunity_Discovery row
- P12 for closeout
Task-specific prompt rules override generic closeout behavior.
Do not substitute a rewritten prompt, plan, or handoff for requested repo work.

prompts.name: Commit-Required Repo-Local Harness Builder
prompts.sprintPathRole: Build and complete a repo-local agent harness
prompts.useThisWhen: A repository lacks, fragments, or has stale agent rules, maps, workflows, registries, validators, scoped skills, hooks, or operator reports.
prompts.doNotUseWhen: The request is planning-only or another prompt owns a narrower non-harness mutation.
prompts.inspectFirst: Existing repo rules, docs, scripts, tests, validators, manifests, hooks, generated-output policy, branch and PR conventions.
prompts.expectedOutput: Implemented and validated repo-local harness contributions committed to the relevant repository.
prompts.nextStep: Run P11 or P12 after the harness commit and PR update.
prompts.acceptanceGate: Changed tracked files, validators pass, commit exists, and push or PR state is reported.
prompts.authority: Full owned harness and Git/PR authority
prompts.proofCeiling: Static and repository validation plus commit/push evidence; runtime proof remains separate where applicable.
prompts.text:
Every serious app should develop a repo-local AI harness.
Do not treat prompts as the harness. Prompts are one artifact inside it.
The harness should include:
- repo agent rules
- codebase map
- workflow specs
- run context
- artifact registry
- validators
- local hooks where useful
- scoped skills
- read-only code intelligence where useful
- English/operator reports
- final handoff compression
Before inventing, inspect existing repo rules, test runners, validators, docs, scripts, manifests, generated-output policy, and branch/PR conventions.
Goal: complete the harness, validate the owned changes, commit and push the coherent contributions, and report the resulting Git or PR evidence; do not stop at a plan, description, prompt pack, or handoff.
A fresh agent should enter the repo, inspect the harness, choose the right workflow, run the right validators, avoid known traps, produce artifacts, and hand off cleanly.
